Redux Toolkit Query (RTK Query) — Complete Guide to Cache Management with Tags (tagTypes, providesTags, invalidatesTags) Step by Step

7 min read
Just now

--

Introduction
In the previous articles, we learned how to configure Redux Toolkit Query (RTK Query), fetch data using Queries, and create new data using Mutations. At this point, you can build applications that communicate with an API using GET and POST requests.
However, one important question still remains.
Imagine you have a page displaying a list of users.
When you add a new user successfully, what should happen?
Should the list automatically update?
Or should the user manually refresh the page?
Similarly, suppose you update a user’s information or delete a product from an inventory. Should your application still display the old cached data?
Of course not.
The UI should always display the latest information from the server.
This is where RTK Query Tags become one of the framework’s most powerful features.
Tags allow RTK Query to understand which cached data has become outdated and automatically refetch fresh data when necessary.
Without Tags, developers often write manual refetch logic, dispatch additional actions, or reload pages after every mutation. RTK Query eliminates this boilerplate by providing a clean and automatic cache invalidation system.
In this article, you’ll learn everything about:

• What caching is

• Why caching exists

• Why stale data becomes a problem

• What Tags are

• tagTypes

• providesTags

• invalidatesTags

• Automatic refetching

• Cache invalidation

• Cache lifetime

• Refetch options

• Best practices used in production applications
Every concept is explained step by step with practical examples.
Understanding Caching
Before learning Tags, you first need to understand Caching.
Imagine your application fetches all users.
GET /usersThe server responds with:
[
{
"id": 1,
"name": "Ali"
},
{
"id": 2,
"name": "Ahmed"
}
]Instead of requesting this data every time the component renders, RTK Query stores it in memory.
This stored copy is called the Cache.
The next time another component requests the same endpoint, RTK Query simply returns the cached data instead of making another network request.
Benefits include:

• Faster applications

• Reduced API requests

• Better performance

• Improved user experience

• Less server load
Caching is one of the biggest reasons RTK Query performs so well.
The Problem with Caching
Although caching is beneficial, it introduces another challenge.
Suppose the cached data contains:
Ali
AhmedNow another user adds:
Johnto the database.
Your cache still contains only:
Ali
AhmedYour application is now displaying outdated information.
This is called Stale Cache.
RTK Query needs a way to know that the cached data is no longer valid.
That’s exactly what Tags solve.
What Are Tags?
A Tag is simply a label attached to cached data.
Think of it as a name that identifies a particular cache.
Example:
Usersor
Productsor
PostsWhenever data changes, RTK Query checks which Tag is affected.
If the Tag becomes invalid, RTK Query automatically refetches the latest data from the server.
No manual refresh required.
How Tags Work
The complete process looks like this:
GET Users
│
▼
Cache Users
│
▼
Tag = Users
│
▼
POST User
│
▼
Invalidate Users Tag
│
▼
Automatic Refetch
│
▼
Updated UIThis entire workflow happens automatically.
Step 1 — Define tagTypes
Inside your API Slice:
export const api = createApi({ reducerPath: "api", baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({
baseUrl: "...",
}), tagTypes: ["Users"], endpoints: (builder) => ({
}),});Understanding tagTypes
tagTypes: ["Users"],Before RTK Query can use Tags, we must tell it which Tags exist.
Here we define one Tag named:
UsersYou can define multiple Tags.
Example:
tagTypes: [
"Users",
"Posts",
"Products",
"Orders",
]Think of tagTypes as registering categories of cache that your application manages.
Step 2 — providesTags
Now create a GET endpoint.
getUsers: builder.query({ query: () => "users", providesTags: ["Users"],}),What Does providesTags Mean?
Whenever this Query runs successfully, RTK Query stores its response inside the cache.
This line:
providesTags: ["Users"]attaches the Users Tag to that cached response.
Think of it as saying:
“This cached data belongs to the Users category.”
Now RTK Query knows exactly which cache this query created.
Step 3 — invalidatesTags
Now let’s create a Mutation.
addUser: builder.mutation({ query: (newUser) => ({ url: "users", method: "POST", body: newUser, }), invalidatesTags: ["Users"],}),What Does invalidatesTags Mean?
Suppose a user creates a new record.
The database changes.
Our cached Users list is now outdated.
This line:
invalidatesTags: ["Users"]tells RTK Query:
“The Users cache is no longer valid.”
Immediately after the Mutation succeeds, RTK Query automatically performs:
GET /usersagain.
Your UI updates automatically.
No refresh button.
No manual dispatch.
No additional API call inside React.
Everything happens automatically.
Complete Example
export const api = createApi({ reducerPath: "api", baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({
baseUrl: "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/",
}), tagTypes: ["Users"], endpoints: (builder) => ({ getUsers: builder.query({ query: () => "users", providesTags: ["Users"], }), addUser: builder.mutation({ query: (user) => ({
url: "users",
method: "POST",
body: user,
}), invalidatesTags: ["Users"], }), }),});This is the most common pattern you’ll use in production applications.
What Happens Internally?
Imagine this sequence.
App Starts
│
▼
GET Users
│
▼
Cache Created
│
▼
Tag Attached
│
▼
User Adds New User
│
▼
Mutation Success
│
▼
Users Tag Invalidated
│
▼
Automatic Refetch
│
▼
Cache Updated
│
▼
React Re-rendersThis predictable cycle keeps your UI synchronized with the server.
Dynamic Tags
Large applications often cache individual records.
Instead of:
providesTags: ["Users"]you can provide Tags dynamically.
Example:
providesTags: (result) => result
? [
...result.map(({ id }) => ({
type: "Users",
id,
})),
{ type: "Users", id: "LIST" },
]
: [{ type: "Users", id: "LIST" }]Now each user has its own Tag.
Example:
Users-1Users-2Users-3This allows RTK Query to refresh only one user instead of refetching the entire list.
This technique is commonly used in enterprise applications.
Cache Lifetime
RTK Query doesn’t keep cached data forever.
By default:
60 SecondsAfter the last component stops using the query, RTK Query waits 60 seconds before removing it from memory.
You can customize this.
Example:
keepUnusedDataFor: 120Now cached data stays for:
120 SecondsLonger cache lifetimes reduce unnecessary API requests but may increase memory usage.
Choose a value that matches your application’s needs.
Refetch Options
Sometimes you want RTK Query to refetch data under specific conditions.
Refetch on Mount
useGetUsersQuery(undefined, { refetchOnMountOrArgChange: true,});Whenever the component mounts again, RTK Query fetches fresh data instead of relying solely on the cache.
Refetch on Window Focus
refetchOnFocus: trueImagine the user switches to another browser tab.
While they are away, another user updates the data.
When the original user returns, RTK Query automatically refetches the latest information.
Refetch on Reconnect
refetchOnReconnect: trueSuppose the internet connection disappears.
When the connection returns, RTK Query automatically requests fresh data.
No manual retry logic required.
When Should You Use Tags?
Tags are especially useful whenever data changes.
Examples include:

• User Management

• Product Management

• Shopping Cart

• Orders

• Blog Posts

• Comments

• Notifications

• Inventory Systems
If your application performs Create, Update, or Delete operations, you should almost always use Tags.
Common Beginner Mistakes
Many beginners encounter the same issues when first working with RTK Query Tags. Here are the most common ones:

• Forgetting to define tagTypes in createApi().

• Using invalidatesTags without a matching providesTags.

• Misspelling Tag names (for example, "User" vs "Users"). Tag names are case-sensitive and must match exactly.

• Expecting a query to refetch automatically without invalidating the relevant Tag.

• Invalidating every Tag in the application when only one resource changed, leading to unnecessary network requests.

• Forgetting that mutations don’t automatically update cached queries unless you configure cache invalidation or update the cache manually.
Checking these points first can save a lot of debugging time.
Best Practices
As your application grows, using Tags consistently becomes increasingly important. Here are some best practices followed in production applications:

• Define all your tagTypes at the top of your API Slice to keep them centralized.

• Use plural, descriptive names such as "Users", "Posts", and "Products" for list resources.

• For large datasets, prefer dynamic Tags with IDs so that only the affected item is refreshed instead of refetching the entire collection.

• Use invalidatesTags only for resources that actually changed.

• Combine Tags with keepUnusedDataFor and refetch options to balance freshness and performance.

• Organize related endpoints within a single API Slice so they can share Tags effectively.
Following these practices will keep your API layer efficient, predictable, and easier to maintain.
Key Takeaways
Let’s summarize the most important concepts from this article:

• Caching stores API responses so they can be reused without making unnecessary network requests.

• Cached data can become stale when the underlying data changes on the server.

• Tags act as labels that identify cached data.

• tagTypes registers the types of Tags your API Slice can use.

• providesTags attaches Tags to cached query results.

• invalidatesTags marks those Tags as outdated after a mutation.

• Once a Tag is invalidated, RTK Query automatically refetches any active queries associated with that Tag.

• Features such as dynamic Tags, cache lifetime, and automatic refetch options give you fine-grained control over how and when data stays up to date.
Understanding Tags is one of the biggest milestones in learning RTK Query because it allows you to build applications that stay synchronized with the backend automatically, without writing extra state management logic.
What’s Next?
Now that you understand how RTK Query manages caching and automatic updates, the next step is to learn how to build a complete CRUD application using all of these concepts together.
In the next article, we’ll create a production-style project that includes:

• Fetching records with Queries

• Creating records with Mutations

• Updating records using PUT and PATCH

• Deleting records with DELETE

• Dynamic route parameters

• Optimistic updates

• Cache invalidation using Tags

• Error handling and loading states

• Folder organization and project best practices
By the end of the next guide, you’ll understand how all the pieces of RTK Query fit together in a real-world React application.
